The impacts calculated for L-tryptophan and L-valine are to our knowledge the first ones to be published and considered in LCA of pig production. They are very high compared to those of other ingredients and to those of lysine, threonine and methionine (Meul et al., 2012; Mosnier et al., 2011) but we may expect great uncertainty on these values, because published data on resources and emissions of their production processes are scarce. How- ever, this does not influence the findings of our study as demonstrated by the low sensitivity coefficients of our results to the impacts of FU amino acids.

The environmental impacts of pig production calcu- lated in this study were in the range of the values reported in a recent review by de Vries and de Boer (2010). We compared results of the 2P-LowCP scenario with previous studies because this scenario is the most representative of feeding practices for pig production in France (CORPEN, 2003) and Europe. The CC impact in the slurry manage- ment system with average Brazilian soybean (2.25 kg CO2- eq/kg live weight in SOYRAP and 2.51 kg CO2-eq/kg live weight in SOY) was consistent with the values found for conventional pig systems with slurry management: 2.30 kg CO2-eq (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005), 2.30 kg CO2-eq (Rigolot et al., 2009), 2.20 kg CO2-eq (Nguyen et al., 2011), and 2.47 to 3.05 kg CO2-eq depend- ing on the performance level of the system (Pelletier et al., 2010). However, some of the methodological LCA choices in our study differed from those in previous studies. None of the previous studies accounted for the effect of land use change on C release due to conversion of Brazilian forest to cropland according to PAS2050 (2011) as we did. This led to a slightly higher value than in Nguyen et al. (2011), in relation to a much lower CC impact of soybean meal in their study (0.47 VS. 1.47 kg CO2-eq/kg). In the studies of Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005) and Pelletier et al. (2010), this methodological difference was probably com- pensated by their lower values for sow productivity and higher values for feed conversion ratio. The CC impact in the solid manure management system with average Brazilian soybean (3.14 kg CO2-eq in SOYRAP and 3.41 kg CO2-eq in SOY) was also in accordance with pre- vious findings for straw-based systems: 3.46 kg CO2-eq/kg in the Red Label system in Basset-Mens and van der

Werf (2005) and 2.52 and 3.33 kg CO2-eq/kg in high and low-profitability deep-bedded niche systems in Pelletier et al. (2010). Our values for deep litter systems were consistently higher than those for slatted floor systems due to higher GHG emissions during housing and manure storage. The highest GHG emissions were also reported in straw-based systems in a recent review from Reckmann et al. (2012).

The impacts on AC for the 2P-LowCP scenario in slatted floor systems reached 44.9 g SO2-eq/kg live weight and were also consistent with the previous studies: 41.8 to 43.5 SO2-eq/kg live weight (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005; Nguyen et al., 2011; Rigolot, 2009). The values obtained in deep litter systems were lower than in slatted floor systems as already mentioned and reached 33.7 g SO2-eq/kg live weight. This is in accordance with the previous finding of 36.7 g SO2-eq/kg live weight (Rigolot, 2009) but higher than the value of 22.6 SO2-eq/kg live weight of the Red Label system in Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005) relating to outdoor rearing for SOWS and suckling piglets in their study.

Our EU impacts for slatted floor systems reached 19.2 PO4-eq/kg live weight which is in accordance with previous values: 20.8 g PO4-eq/kg live weight in the con- ventional system in Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005); 20.3 PO4-eq/kg live weight in the conventional system without manure treatment in Rigolot (2009); 18.5 g PO4- eq/kg live weight in Nguyen et al. (2011); and 15.9 and 20.8 PO4-eq/kg live weight for high and low-profitability commodity systems in Pelletier et al. (2010). In deep-bedded systems, EU reached 16.7 g PO4-eq/kg live weight which is highly consistent with the value of 16.6 PO4-eq/kg live weight in the Red Label system in Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005) and with the 19.2 PO4-eq/kg live weight in the system with straw bedding for fattening pigs in Rigolot (2009). However, our results are very different from those of Pelletier et al. (2010) who found 32.2 and 45.9 PO4-eq/kg live weight in their deep-bedded niche system. This differ- ence may be related to a much higher value for feed-to-gain ratio of fattening pigs in Pelletier et al. (2010) than in our study (3.63 and 3.96 kg/kg VS. 2.84 kg/kg).

As for terrestrial ecotoxicity, values from the literature are scarcer than for the other impacts. The values in this study varied between 16.6 and 18.5 g 1,4 DCB-eq/kg live weight which are very similar to the values of 16.5 and 18.4 g 1,4 DCB-eq/kg live weight in Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005) and of 19.3 g 1,4 DCB-eq/kg live weight in Rigolot (2009).

Land occupation was on average 4.00 m² year/kg live weight in this study, and similar to the value of 4.4 m² year/kg live weight in Nguyen et al. (2011) and 4.12 m² year/ kg live weight in Dourmad et al. (2012), but lower than the results of Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005) and Rigolot (2009) who respectively found 5.43 and 5.34 m² year/kg live weight for conventional systems. This divergence may be related to differences in terms of animal performance (sow productivity and feed-to-gain ratio) between our study and these works.

CED in the 2P-LowCP scenario ranged between 17.3 and 19.7 MJ/kg live weight which is slightly higher than the previous values: 15.9 MJ/kg live weight for the conven- tional system and 17.9 MJ/kg live weight for the Red Label

system in Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005); 15.2 MJ/ kg live weight in the slatted floor system and MJ/kg live weight in the system with straw bedding for fattening pigs in Rigolot (2009); 13.6 MJ/kg live weight in Nguyen et al. (2011) and 16.2 MJ/kg live weight for the conven- tional system in Dourmad et al. (2012). The much lower values ranging from 9.7 to 11.9 MJ/kg live weight in Pelletier et al. (2010) may be attributed to a difference in CED for soybean meal because in their study soybean originated from the Upper Midwestern United States, 100 km from the feed factory.
4.2. Potential for reducing environmental impacts through feeding practices

The hypothesis of reducing the environmental impacts of pig production through the incorporation of FU amino acids to obtain low-protein diets was clearly validated by our results for CC, AC and EU impacts. Whatever the manure management system, the soybean origin, the availability of protein-rich feed ingredients or the number of feeds for fattening pigs, the incorporation of FU amino acids in the LowCP, Min€ and MinCP scenarios reduced the CC, AC and EU impacts relatively to the NoAA scenario. The lowest CC, AC and EU impacts were reached with the Min€ or MinCP scenarios. These results demonstrate that the lowest protein diets formulated, including not only lysine, threonine and methionine but also tryptophan and valine, were the most efficient in reducing CC, AC and EU impacts. The CED, Terrestrial Ecotoxicity and Land Occupa- tion were reduced in the LowCP and Min€ scenario relatively to NoAA, but to a lesser extent. CED was always reduced by the incorporation of FU amino acids when soybean meal was the sole protein-rich ingredient avail- able and reduced in LowCP and Min€ scenarios when rapeseed meal and pea were also available. Lowest Terres- trial Ecotoxicity was always observed in the Min€ scenar- ios. Land Occupation was slightly reduced by FU amino acid incorporation when rapeseed meal and pea were available for feed formulation. These findings suggest that incorporating FU amino acids leads to a moderate reduc- tion in energy use and terrestrial ecotoxicity and does not affect land occupation. The low sensitivity of the impacts to contexts of feed ingredient costs (except terrestrial ecotoxicity) suggests that the efficiency of FU amino acid incorporation in reducing the impacts is robust to fluctua- tions in feed ingredient prices. The sensitivity analysis also demonstrated that FU amino acid production impacts have a negligible effect on most pig production impacts (less than 2%) except CED (from 7% to 8%).

Supplementing the diet with limiting amino acids and phase feeding are well identified and used in commercial practice as major nutritional strategies to reduce N and P excretion from pigs. This reduces ammonia emissions (Aarnink and Verstegen, 2007) as well as nitrous oxide emissions (Ball and Möhn, 2003). However, our results, illustrate that the incorporation of FU amino acids into low protein balanced diets decreases both the direct emissions of GHG and NH3 from pig production and the indirect emissions associated with feed ingredient production. Several studies have discussed the contribution analysis

for processes in LCAs of pig production (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005; Eriksson et al., 2005; Nguyen et al., 2011; Pelletier et al., 2010), but we did not find any study reporting the breakdown of processes for impacts reduc- tion when testing improvement scenarios (although the figures displayed in Eriksson et al. (2005) allow this calculation). Nevertheless, the contribution of processes to impacts to reduction is likely to be consistent with the contribution of processes to impacts because the highest reduction potential is expected for the biggest contributor to impacts. For CED, Terrestrial Ecotoxicity and Land Occupation for instance, the impact reduction was almost exclusively associated with feed production. This is obvious because feed production usually accounts for 70% to 100% of these impacts (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005; Eriksson et al., 2005; Nguyen et al., 2011). For CC, the contribution of animal housing to impact reduction was higher in solid manure than in slurry systems. Indeed, the reduction of N excretion was the same in both systems, but the resulting reduction of N20 emissions in buildings was higher in solid manure systems than in slurry systems because the emission factor of N-N20 is 0.06 kg/kg for solid manure systems VS. 0.002 kg/kg for slurry systems (IPCC, 2006). The reduction of AC was almost exclusively associated to animal housing and manure management. This observation was associated to the decrease in NH3 emissions obtained in buildings, at storage and during field application when reducing N excretion in the Min€ scenario. This finding was consistent with the contribution analysis for AC reported in Basset- Mens and van der Werf (2005) and in Eriksson et al. (2005). Reduction of EU was associated with all processes (feed production, animal housing and manure manage- ment), because in Min€ soybean meal and extruded soybean were replaced by cereals of lower EU impact and NH3 and NO3 emissions in buildings at storage and during spreading were decreased. These observations highlight that low protein diets are efficient in lowering the environmental burden of pig production because they modify processes at various stages of the production chain.

Most impacts were minimized with the MP-Min€ or the MP-MinCP scenarios, suggesting that combining incorporation of FU amino acids and multiphase feeding leads to a further reduction in the impacts, so that the effects of the two options are additive as previously suggested by Aarnink and Verstegen (2007) and Ball and Möhn (2003). Both of these practices (incorporation of FU amino acids and multiphase feeding) aim at adjusting the feeding supply to the requirements of the animals, in terms of energy and amino acids. Previous studies showed that this strategy allows for better efficiency of nutrient utilization, and consequently lower excretion (Bourdon et al., 1995; Canh et al., 1998; Galassi et al., 2010; Le Bellego et al., 2002; Osada et al., 2011; Portejoie et al., 2004; Quiniou et al., 2011). Consequently, precision feeding of pigs, with dynamic adjustment of individual supply to require- ments with appropriate low-protein diets balanced with FU amino acids is expected to further reduce nutrient excretion (Hauschild et al., 2012; Pomar et al., 2010). Further work is thus needed to estimate the extent to which precision feeding systems, which enable a more precise individual adjustment
of amino acid inputs to be made, lead to a further reduction in the environmental impacts of pig production.

We performed the LCA of pig production for both slurry and solid manure management systems, considering either the average Brazilian soybean meal or the Centre-West Brazilian soybean meal that is fully associated with recent deforestation. The incorporation of FU amino acids com- bined with multiphase feeding was the most efficient in reducing GHG emissions and energy demand when using Centre-West Brazilian soybean meal and managing waste as solid manure. The reduction potential for CC reached up to 38% in the MP-MinCP scenario when using Centre-West Brazilian soybean meal, managing waste as solid manure and formulating feeds without rapeseed meal and pea. This result suggests that these feeding practices may partly compensate for detrimental options in terms of feed ingre- dient selection and in waste management practices.

Considering that most of the SOWS in France are raised in farms using two phase feeding and that about 90% of the animals are raised on slatted systems, we evaluated the potential of improvement relatively to the current praxis in France with 2P-lowCP in SOYRAP and SL situa- tions. Formulating least-cost feeds with FU amino acids combined with multiphase feeding (MP-Min€) rather than the current practices (2P-LowCP) would reduce CC impact by about 1.3%, AC by 17.8% and EU by 12.5%, whilst reducing feeding cost by 4%. This improvement may be reached with an average incorporation of FU amino acids of 5.55 kg/t of feed in MP-Min€ instead of an average incorporation of 3.53 kg/t in 2P-lowCP. When comparing MP-Min€ VS. 2P-NoAA, reduction of the impacts reaches 9.8% for CC, 28.1% for AC and 24.9% for EU This appears to be a substantial decrease for CC, AC and EU, which are major concerns in Brittany, the most important pig pro- duction area in France. Our study gives further insights into the key factors for environmental sustainability of pig production. Indeed, in addition to the feeding practices investigated, the sensitivity analysis performed revealed some of the technical factors to which the environmental impacts are sensitive. Feed conversion ratios of piglets in the post-weaning phase and of fattening pigs and the productivity of SOWS appear to be key factors of the environmental sustainability of pig production as already pointed out by Pelletier et al. (2010).

The CC impact in the solid manure management system with average Brazilian soybean (3.14 kg CO2-eq in SOYRAP and 3.41 kg CO2-eq in SOY) was also in accordance with pre- vious findings for straw-based systems: 3.46 kg CO2-eq/kg in the Red Label system in Basset-Mens and van der

Werf (2005) and 2.52 and 3.33 kg CO2-eq/kg in high and low-profitability deep-bedded niche systems in Pelletier et al. (2010). Our values for deep litter systems were consistently higher than those for slatted floor systems due to higher GHG emissions during housing and manure storage. The highest GHG emissions were also reported in straw-based systems in a recent review from Reckmann et al. (2012).

The impacts on AC for the 2P-LowCP scenario in slatted floor systems reached 44.9 g SO2-eq/kg live weight and were also consistent with the previous studies: 41.8 to 43.5 SO2-eq/kg live weight (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005; Nguyen et al., 2011; Rigolot, 2009). The values obtained in deep litter systems were lower than in slatted floor systems as already mentioned and reached 33.7 g SO2-eq/kg live weight. This is in accordance with the previous finding of 36.7 g SO2-eq/kg live weight (Rigolot, 2009) but higher than the value of 22.6 SO2-eq/kg live weight of the Red Label system in Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005) relating to outdoor rearing for SOWS and suckling piglets in their study.

Our EU impacts for slatted floor systems reached 19.2 PO4-eq/kg live weight which is in accordance with previous values: 20.8 g PO4-eq/kg live weight in the con- ventional system in Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005); 20.3 PO4-eq/kg live weight in the conventional system without manure treatment in Rigolot (2009); 18.5 g PO4- eq/kg live weight in Nguyen et al. (2011); and 15.9 and 20.8 PO4-eq/kg live weight for high and low-profitability commodity systems in Pelletier et al. (2010). In deep-bedded systems, EU reached 16.7 g PO4-eq/kg live weight which is highly consistent with the value of 16.6 PO4-eq/kg live weight in the Red Label system in Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005) and with the 19.2 PO4-eq/kg live weight in the system with straw bedding for fattening pigs in Rigolot (2009). However, our results are very different from those of Pelletier et al. (2010) who found 32.2 and 45.9 PO4-eq/kg live weight in their deep-bedded niche system. This differ- ence may be related to a much higher value for feed-to-gain ratio of fattening pigs in Pelletier et al. (2010) than in our study (3.63 and 3.96 kg/kg VS. 2.84 kg/kg).

As for terrestrial ecotoxicity, values from the literature are scarcer than for the other impacts. The values in this study varied between 16.6 and 18.5 g 1,4 DCB-eq/kg live weight which are very similar to the values of 16.5 and 18.4 g 1,4 DCB-eq/kg live weight in Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005) and of 19.3 g 1,4 DCB-eq/kg live weight in Rigolot (2009).

Land occupation was on average 4.00 m² year/kg live weight in this study, and similar to the value of 4.4 m² year/kg live weight in Nguyen et al. (2011) and 4.12 m² year/ kg live weight in Dourmad et al. (2012), but lower than the results of Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005) and Rigolot (2009) who respectively found 5.43 and 5.34 m² year/kg live weight for conventional systems. This divergence may be related to differences in terms of animal performance (sow productivity and feed-to-gain ratio) between our study and these works.

CED in the 2P-LowCP scenario ranged between 17.3 and 19.7 MJ/kg live weight which is slightly higher than the previous values: 15.9 MJ/kg live weight for the conven- tional system and 17.9 MJ/kg live weight for the Red Label

system in Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005); 15.2 MJ/ kg live weight in the slatted floor system and MJ/kg live weight in the system with straw bedding for fattening pigs in Rigolot (2009); 13.6 MJ/kg live weight in Nguyen et al. (2011) and 16.2 MJ/kg live weight for the conven- tional system in Dourmad et al. (2012). The much lower values ranging from 9.7 to 11.9 MJ/kg live weight in Pelletier et al. (2010) may be attributed to a difference in CED for soybean meal because in their study soybean originated from the Upper Midwestern United States, 100 km from the feed factory.
4.2. Potential for reducing environmental impacts through feeding practices

The hypothesis of reducing the environmental impacts of pig production through the incorporation of FU amino acids to obtain low-protein diets was clearly validated by our results for CC, AC and EU impacts. Whatever the manure management system, the soybean origin, the availability of protein-rich feed ingredients or the number of feeds for fattening pigs, the incorporation of FU amino acids in the LowCP, Min€ and MinCP scenarios reduced the CC, AC and EU impacts relatively to the NoAA scenario. The lowest CC, AC and EU impacts were reached with the Min€ or MinCP scenarios. These results demonstrate that the lowest protein diets formulated, including not only lysine, threonine and methionine but also tryptophan and valine, were the most efficient in reducing CC, AC and EU impacts. The CED, Terrestrial Ecotoxicity and Land Occupa- tion were reduced in the LowCP and Min€ scenario relatively to NoAA, but to a lesser extent. CED was always reduced by the incorporation of FU amino acids when soybean meal was the sole protein-rich ingredient avail- able and reduced in LowCP and Min€ scenarios when rapeseed meal and pea were also available. Lowest Terres- trial Ecotoxicity was always observed in the Min€ scenar- ios. Land Occupation was slightly reduced by FU amino acid incorporation when rapeseed meal and pea were available for feed formulation. These findings suggest that incorporating FU amino acids leads to a moderate reduc- tion in energy use and terrestrial ecotoxicity and does not affect land occupation. The low sensitivity of the impacts to contexts of feed ingredient costs (except terrestrial ecotoxicity) suggests that the efficiency of FU amino acid incorporation in reducing the impacts is robust to fluctua- tions in feed ingredient prices. The sensitivity analysis also demonstrated that FU amino acid production impacts have a negligible effect on most pig production impacts (less than 2%) except CED (from 7% to 8%).

Supplementing the diet with limiting amino acids and phase feeding are well identified and used in commercial practice as major nutritional strategies to reduce N and P excretion from pigs. This reduces ammonia emissions (Aarnink and Verstegen, 2007) as well as nitrous oxide emissions (Ball and Möhn, 2003). However, our results, illustrate that the incorporation of FU amino acids into low protein balanced diets decreases both the direct emissions of GHG and NH3 from pig production and the indirect emissions associated with feed ingredient production. Several studies have discussed the contribution analysis

for processes in LCAs of pig production (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005; Eriksson et al., 2005; Nguyen et al., 2011; Pelletier et al., 2010), but we did not find any study reporting the breakdown of processes for impacts reduc- tion when testing improvement scenarios (although the figures displayed in Eriksson et al. (2005) allow this calculation). Nevertheless, the contribution of processes to impacts to reduction is likely to be consistent with the contribution of processes to impacts because the highest reduction potential is expected for the biggest contributor to impacts. For CED, Terrestrial Ecotoxicity and Land Occupation for instance, the impact reduction was almost exclusively associated with feed production. This is obvious because feed production usually accounts for 70% to 100% of these impacts (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005; Eriksson et al., 2005; Nguyen et al., 2011). For CC, the contribution of animal housing to impact reduction was higher in solid manure than in slurry systems. Indeed, the reduction of N excretion was the same in both systems, but the resulting reduction of N20 emissions in buildings was higher in solid manure systems than in slurry systems because the emission factor of N-N20 is 0.06 kg/kg for solid manure systems VS. 0.002 kg/kg for slurry systems (IPCC, 2006). The reduction of AC was almost exclusively associated to animal housing and manure management. This observation was associated to the decrease in NH3 emissions obtained in buildings, at storage and during field application when reducing N excretion in the Min€ scenario. This finding was consistent with the contribution analysis for AC reported in Basset- Mens and van der Werf (2005) and in Eriksson et al. (2005). Reduction of EU was associated with all processes (feed production, animal housing and manure manage- ment), because in Min€ soybean meal and extruded soybean were replaced by cereals of lower EU impact and NH3 and NO3 emissions in buildings at storage and during spreading were decreased. These observations highlight that low protein diets are efficient in lowering the environmental burden of pig production because they modify processes at various stages of the production chain.

Most impacts were minimized with the MP-Min€ or the MP-MinCP scenarios, suggesting that combining incorporation of FU amino acids and multiphase feeding leads to a further reduction in the impacts, so that the effects of the two options are additive as previously suggested by Aarnink and Verstegen (2007) and Ball and Möhn (2003). Both of these practices (incorporation of FU amino acids and multiphase feeding) aim at adjusting the feeding supply to the requirements of the animals, in terms of energy and amino acids. Previous studies showed that this strategy allows for better efficiency of nutrient utilization, and consequently lower excretion (Bourdon et al., 1995; Canh et al., 1998; Galassi et al., 2010; Le Bellego et al., 2002; Osada et al., 2011; Portejoie et al., 2004; Quiniou et al., 2011). Consequently, precision feeding of pigs, with dynamic adjustment of individual supply to require- ments with appropriate low-protein diets balanced with FU amino acids is expected to further reduce nutrient excretion (Hauschild et al., 2012; Pomar et al., 2010). Further work is thus needed to estimate the extent to which precision feeding systems, which enable a more precise individual adjustment
of amino acid inputs to be made, lead to a further reduction in the environmental impacts of pig production.

We performed the LCA of pig production for both slurry and solid manure management systems, considering either the average Brazilian soybean meal or the Centre-West Brazilian soybean meal that is fully associated with recent deforestation. The incorporation of FU amino acids com- bined with multiphase feeding was the most efficient in reducing GHG emissions and energy demand when using Centre-West Brazilian soybean meal and managing waste as solid manure. The reduction potential for CC reached up to 38% in the MP-MinCP scenario when using Centre-West Brazilian soybean meal, managing waste as solid manure and formulating feeds without rapeseed meal and pea. This result suggests that these feeding practices may partly compensate for detrimental options in terms of feed ingre- dient selection and in waste management practices.

Considering that most of the SOWS in France are raised in farms using two phase feeding and that about 90% of the animals are raised on slatted systems, we evaluated the potential of improvement relatively to the current praxis in France with 2P-lowCP in SOYRAP and SL situa- tions. Formulating least-cost feeds with FU amino acids combined with multiphase feeding (MP-Min€) rather than the current practices (2P-LowCP) would reduce CC impact by about 1.3%, AC by 17.8% and EU by 12.5%, whilst reducing feeding cost by 4%. This improvement may be reached with an average incorporation of FU amino acids of 5.55 kg/t of feed in MP-Min€ instead of an average incorporation of 3.53 kg/t in 2P-lowCP. When comparing MP-Min€ VS. 2P-NoAA, reduction of the impacts reaches 9.8% for CC, 28.1% for AC and 24.9% for EU This appears to be a substantial decrease for CC, AC and EU, which are major concerns in Brittany, the most important pig pro- duction area in France. Our study gives further insights into the key factors for environmental sustainability of pig production. Indeed, in addition to the feeding practices investigated, the sensitivity analysis performed revealed some of the technical factors to which the environmental impacts are sensitive. Feed conversion ratios of piglets in the post-weaning phase and of fattening pigs and the productivity of SOWS appear to be key factors of the environmental sustainability of pig production as already pointed out by Pelletier et al. (2010).
22: <header>4.1. Environmental impacts of feed ingredients and of pig production </header>

The impacts calculated for most of the feed ingredients were close to those previously found by Mosnier et al. (2011) and Nguyen et al. (2012a, 2012b). A major update was made for soybean meal, taking into account the effect of land-use change on C release due to conversion of Brazilian forest to cropland (PAS2050, 2011). This modifi- cation doubled the CC impact of soybean meal and con- tributed substantially to increasing the absolute values of our estimations for pig production impact on CC. The impacts calculated for L-tryptophan and L-valine are to our knowledge the first ones to be published and considered in LCA of pig production. They are very high compared to those of other ingredients and to those of lysine, threonine and methionine (Meul et al., 2012; Mosnier et al., 2011) but we may expect great uncertainty on these values, because published data on resources and emissions of their production processes are scarce. How- ever, this does not influence the findings of our study as demonstrated by the low sensitivity coefficients of our results to the impacts of FU amino acids.

The environmental impacts of pig production calcu- lated in this study were in the range of the values reported in a recent review by de Vries and de Boer (2010). We compared results of the 2P-LowCP scenario with previous studies because this scenario is the most representative of feeding practices for pig production in France (CORPEN, 2003) and Europe. 23: The incorporation of FU amino acids associated with a decrease in crude protein content of diets contributes to reducing the impacts of pig production on climate change, acidification and eutrophication. As for current pig feeding practices in France, there is still a major potential to reduce environmental impacts at low cost, especially for acidifica- tion (17.8%), eutrophication (12.5%) and climate change (1.3%). Indeed, the incorporation of recently available FU amino acids such as tryptophan and valine in pig feeds together with multiphase feeding allows a further reduc- tion of the impacts relatively to the current practices. The underlying mechanisms affect various stages of the pro- duction chain such as feed production, animal housing and

manure management. They include the substitution of soybean meal and extruded soybean by cereals and FU amino acids and the reduction of nitrogen excretion which further reduces nitrous oxide and ammonia emissions. The incorporation of FU amino acids together with multi- phase feeding appears to be a win win strategy because it allows mitigation of several impacts. Further work is needed to estimate the potential reduction of the environmental impact of pig production with individual precision feeding.
